input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin is assembled from a heterogeneous portfolio of computational models documented in MBSE artifacts. Structural information is captured with SysML block and internal block diagrams; functional and logical architectures are expressed via ADLs such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, or SysML IBDs. Behavioural aspects are modelled using SysML state‑machine diagrams (v1.7) or MontiArc Automaton, and are linked to executable simulation models implemented in MathWorks Simulink (white‑box, physics‑based) or to data‑driven black‑box models such as machine‑learning regressors and probabilistic latent‑variable models. Input data traces collected from the cyber‑physical system are consumed by a Processing component that applies the simulation or calculation models and emits derived output traces, which become part of the digital shadow. Model relationships are recorded through the 'originates‑from' association, allowing full traceability from each output trace back to the generating processing component, the input traces, and the specific models used. Model management follows a reference‑model‑concretization workflow: a Digital Shadow Reference Model is instantiated with concrete classes and mappings, ensuring conformance to the reference class diagram.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is ensured by combining analytical white‑box models (e.g., Simulink dynamic simulations) with data‑driven black‑box models (e.g., machine‑learning based friction representations) to capture both known physics and complex nonlinear effects. Validation relies on traceability links (originates‑from) that allow verification of model‑to‑model transformations, such as from SysML specifications to simulation models. Uncertainty sources include inaccuracies in input data traces and model parameter estimation; these are addressed through traceability‑based error detection and corrective updates. Quality assurance is emphasized through verification of transformation correctness and the maintenance of metadata that records model provenance and data lineage.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The system implements an automated one‑way data flow from the physical asset to the digital representation. Sensor measurements are streamed from the physical object using the MQTT protocol (Mosquitto server implementation) as described in the cited sources. The incoming MQTT messages constitute contextual data traces that are aggregated and abstracted within the digital shadow model. No explicit transmission frequency, event‑trigger conditions, or preprocessing steps are detailed in the documents; the flow is defined as a passive, continuous streaming of sensor data into the digital shadow.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: According to the digital shadow reference model, the digital shadow (and thus the described Digital Twin implementation) functions as a passive entity that does not operate autonomously nor push data back to the physical system. Consequently, there is no virtual‑to‑physical control flow, no command messages, and no validation, authentication, or feedback mechanisms defined for sending instructions to the physical asset.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle follows the MBSE development hierarchy described in Document 1: requirements modeling, functional‑architecture modeling, logical‑architecture modeling, and product/technical‑layer modeling. At each stage the representation is a model in a specific language (e.g., SysML requirement diagrams, SysML internal‑block diagrams, ADL component‑connector models such as ACME or AADL, and finally executable simulation models in Simulink or C++). Transitions between stages are managed by refining abstract models into more concrete ones and by maintaining traceability links defined in the reference model (see Document 2). Lifecycle governance is implied by the need to keep the concrete realization conformant to the reference model throughout development.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Engineering of the Digital Twin uses the MBSE approach (Document 1) together with methodology families such as RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT. Models are created in SysML, ADLs (ACME, AADL, MontiArc) and linked to simulation tools (Simulink) or source code (C++, Java). Evolution is controlled by the conformance relation defined in the Digital Shadow Reference Model (Document 2); concrete realizations are mapped to reference concepts, allowing version‑controlled updates while preserving the “originates‑from” traceability of data traces to processing components and models.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: A Processing component (Document 2) consumes input data traces, applies data‑calculation or simulation models, and emits output data that constitute the digital shadow. The processing logic provides fine‑grained traceability of each output to its input traces, the responsible component, and the models used, thereby enabling decision‑support analytics such as computed higher‑level abstractions. Confidence can be inferred from the preserved traceability chain, although explicit confidence metrics are not described.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The approach relies on an internal reference‑model standardisation mechanism: the Digital Shadow reference model defines domain concepts and their relationships, and a conformance relation explicitly identifies which concrete class diagrams (CDs) are valid realizations. Concrete realizations are produced by mapping concrete elements to reference concepts, ensuring semantic alignment across models. This internal conformance and the originates‑from traceability association serve as the primary standardisation and interoperability framework described in the documents. No external industry standards, certifications or interoperability specifications are mentioned.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

[62] Z. Huang, Y. Shen, J. Li, M. Fey, and C. Brecher, “A survey on aidriven digital twins in industry 4.0: Smart manufacturing and advanced
robotics,” _Sensors_, vol. 21, no. 19, 2021.

[63] M. Liebenberg and M. Jarke, “Information systems engineering with
digital shadows: Concept and case studies,” in _Advanced Information_
_Systems Engineering_, S. Dustdar, E. Yu, C. Salinesi, D. Rieu, and
V. Pant, Eds. Cham: Springer International Publishing, 2020, pp. 70–84.

[64] P. Sapel, A. Gannouni, J. Fulterer, C. Hopmann, M. Schmitz, D. Lütticke,
A. Gützlaff, and G. Schuh, “Towards digital shadows for production
planning and control in injection molding,” _CIRP Journal of Manufac-_
_turing Science and Technology_, vol. 38, pp. 243–251, 2022.

[65] C. Idzik, D. Hilger, N. Hosters, M. Kemmerling, P. Niemietz,
L. Ortjohann, J. Sasse, A. Serafeim, J. Wang, D. Wolff, and G. Hirt,
_Decision Support for the Optimization of Continuous Processes using_
_Digital Shadows_ . Cham: Springer International Publishing, 2023, pp.
[1–21. [Online]. Available: https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-98062-7_](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-98062-7_12-2)
[12-2](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-98062-7_12-2)

[66] H. M. Muctadir, D. A. Manrique Negrin, R. Gunasekaran, L. Cleophas,
M. van den Brand, and B. R. Haverkort, “Current trends in digital
twin development, maintenance, and operation: an interview study,”
_Software and Systems Modeling_, vol. 23, no. 5, pp. 1275–1305, Oct
[2024. [Online]. Available: https://doi.org/10.1007/s10270-024-01167-z](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10270-024-01167-z)

nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

This DS structure is described as a reference model on

purpose: A digital shadow designer has the flexibility to
concretize the Digital Shadow Reference Model and customize
the DS model to suit their specific use case, as we do in this
paper. _Reference models_ describe concepts of a domain and
their relationships, and they have “ _a conformance relation that_
_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:

block_1_judge.reasoning: The source document provides a detailed description of the physical system under study. Quotes: 'The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes', 'first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor', and it is 'suitable for complex manufacturing tasks such as machining'.

The document describes the specific services and capabilities provided by the digital shadow, including visualization and analysis. Quotes: 'live-data linking as well as visualization in a dashboard', 'enable the continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction', and 'enhance the robot’s motion accuracy'.

The document explicitly lists various tools, technologies, and frameworks used to implement the digital shadow. Quotes: 'implementing Asset Administration Shells (AAS)', 'SysML', 'MathWorks Simulink', 'Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl', and 'streamed via MQTT'.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document'. While the text mentions general temporal concepts like 'real-time' and 'live data', it does not provide the specific technical details required by the characteristic definition, such as 'synchronization frequencies', 'latency requirements', or specific 'temporal granularity'. Therefore, the detailed characteristic is considered absent from the text.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text focuses on the creation of a single digital shadow ('FrictionShadow') for the first axis of a robot. It mentions 'different types of models' and 'several data series', but does not describe 'multiple digital twin instances', 'centralized vs decentralized architectures', or coordination between twins. Thus, the characteristic is absent.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The text describes a 'Digital Shadow Reference Model' and a 'concrete realization' of a single shadow instance (defined via UML and SysML). It does not describe the 'overall orchestration' of a broader Digital Twin system, 'system-wide coordination mechanisms', or 'resource management' patterns typically associated with a 'constellation'.

Although the document mentions 'Asset Administration Shells (AAS)' and 'MQTT', these are described as components and protocols used within the specific digital shadow implementation for the robot (vertical integration). The text does not describe integration with external enterprise systems, other digital twins, or system-wide interoperability patterns outside of this specific loop.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

_B. Relations to the state-of-the-art_


This work is situated in the dynamic field of digital representations, with a particular focus on purpose-driven digital


shadows. While the overarching concept of these digital representations has been a subject of extensive research, recent
efforts focus on systematic, application-specific methodologies
to address concrete industrial challenges [56], [57]. This digital
shadow approach provides a general framework for data-driven
modeling of complex physical effects, while being directly
applicable to industrial robot friction. It aligns with recent
advances in scalable identification methods, such as methods
by Sorrentino et al. [14], which avoid dedicated setups and
leverage intrinsic robot models and state data. Adaptive control
and probabilistic latent variable models further exemplify the
field’s move toward capturing nonlinear friction phenomena
more accurately than traditional static models.
A key challenge in creating effective digital shadows is the
integration of diverse modeling paradigms. Many industrial
processes, including robot dynamics, require a combination
of physics-based (white-box) models and data-driven (blackbox) models. A white-box model, such as a Simulink-based
dynamic simulation, offers clear interpretation and predictability [58]. Conversely, data-driven approaches, such as machine
learning models, can capture complex, non-linear relationships that are difficult to represent with analytical models

[58]. Current research emphasizes the need to combine these
complementary strengths to create more comprehensive and
accurate digital shadows [58], [59].
The reliability of a digital shadow is directly dependent on
the correctness of its underlying models and the transformations applied to the data. Research highlights the importance of
traceability and verification in the model-based development of
complex systems, proposing methods to ensure the correctness
of transformations (e.g., from SysML to simulation models)

nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and

the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data

_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_

Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.

Quotes: 'modeling is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g., requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layer.'; 'models are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as MathWorks Simulink [...] or code in general-purpose languages like C++ or Java.'; 'a conformance relation that precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the same language'.

The extraction precisely details the MBSE approach and specific methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM, etc.) and tools (SysML, ACME, AADL) found in Section II.A. The extraction correctly describes the Processing component's role in consuming/producing traces and applying models (Section II.B) and the fine-grained traceability enabled. It mentions 'decision-support analytics' and 'confidence' which are interpretative but grounded in the text's discussion of 'semantic support' and 'better understanding' (Section I, II.B). The technical description of the processing logic is strong. Quotes: 'Processing component [...] consumes and produces data traces and processes data calculation and simulation models'; 'simulation and data calculation models help compute higher-level abstractions.'; 'supporting fine-grained traceability'.

The extraction correctly identifies the 'Digital Shadow Reference Model' as the internal standardization mechanism, detailing the conformance relation and the concept of 'concrete realization' found in Section II.B. It accurately notes that the text focuses on this internal framework rather than external industry standards.

data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:
